item.title: [41625] VA-240110-BKT EXHIBIT D- STATEMENT OF WORK CHANGE ORDER TEMPLATE
[41625] Privacy Impact Assessment for the Electronic Surveillance System (ELSUR) DHS
item.description: ELSUR is owned by the ICE Office of Homeland Security Investigations (HSI). ELSUR allows ICE to capture and search for information included in applications for court orders authorizing ICE to intercept oral, wire, or electronic communications, such as telephone conversations and emails. When ICE is engaged in joint investigations with state or municipal law enforcement agencies, ICE agents may also support requests filed with state courts for electronic surveillance under the applicable state’s electronic surveillance laws. These requests will be captured in ELSUR as well. ELSUR tracks these federal and state electronic surveillance requests whether or not they are granted by the court. ELSUR also supports the sharing of this information within ICE and with other federal investigative agencies as required by Title III. | 19 pages | Source: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement

item.title: [1255] US DIS DCD 1 25cv105 d5474370e4127 Joint MOTION to Approve Consent Judgment by SECURI
[296] Honolulu EMS Arbitration Decisions and Investigations
[343] Superior Court Judge Una Gandbhir Order on Campbell STEM lawsuit
[727] Mifepristone order
item.description: 5th US Circuit Court of Appeals | 19 pages | Source: CNN

item.title: [38126] U.S. Court of Appeals Ruling on Pretrial Detention of Brian Cole Jr.
[1079] Judge extends temporary sealing order related to request for special prosecutor
item.description: New York State Supreme Court Justice Paul Wojtaszek on April 29 extended a temporary sealing order for the Legal Aid Bureau of Buffalo's motion to disqualify the Erie County District Attorney's Office and name a special prosecutor in the case involving allegations of misconduct at the District Attorney's Office and the Buffalo Police Department. | 5 pages

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00020 - Employment and Social Development Canada
item.description: When posting ads on the Job Bank, one could choose to post for 28 days upon first posting. That has now changed to 21 days. I want to see the directives which precipitated this change, including any and all rationale behind this change, any directives given and any discussions or papers or reports or statistics to rationalize this change, which appears very arbitrary. Ensure to include the names of those who made the decision, and any meetings that were had prior to making this change. (Time frame 2025-01-01 to 2025-12-04) | 71 pages | Source: Employment and Social Development Canada

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2024-03299 - Employment and Social Development Canada
item.description: Pursuant to section 75 of the Employment Insurance Regulations (SOR/96-332), “75. An employer may appeal to the Commission a decision made pursuant to section 72 of these Regulations or pursuant to subsection 69(4) of the Act or a new decision made pursuant to subsection 69(5) of the Act, within one year after the day on which notice of the decision or new decision is sent to the employer.” We wish to read the employer appeals to the CEIC. More specifically, we wish to read the CEIC decisions that refer to, analyze or consider subsection 63(b) of the Employment Insurance Regulations (SOR/96-332) | 152 pages | Source: Employment and Social Development Canada

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00390 - Privy Council Office
item.description: 2024 and 2025 minutes of the Committee on Internal Trade, records of decisions and attached documentation. Include any changes made to the operation committee | 27 pages | Source: Privy Council Office

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00299 - Privy Council Office
item.description: Copies of all briefing documents, memoranda, transition books, information notes, or any other written documents prepared for Michael Sabia in preparation for his appointment as Clerk of the Privy Council and Secretary of the Cabinet position he has held since July 7, 2025. Period covered: June 1, 2025 to July 11, 2025, inclusive. Documents provided to Mr. Sabia to prepare him for his duties and to inform him of the important files and issues within his mandate. This includes any document produced or transmitted by the Privy Council Office, by the former Clerk John Hannaford (in office until July 4, 2025), or from any other government department, agency or entity. | 138 pages | Source: Privy Council Office

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2024-49744 - Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada
item.description: For the period January 1, 2020, to December 31, 2024, please provide copies of briefing notes, discussions/opinion/position papers, manuals, policies and directives, reports, and standard operating procedures related to the IRCC's request, reason for request, receipt and use of DNA and other genetic information (genetic testing, DNA testing, hereditary testing, or sequencing information). Please provide copies of statistical data that is already prepared with regards to (1) the total number of dependent permanent resident applications received by the IRCC from January 1, 2000, to February 18, 2025. (2) all requests by the IRCC for DNA testing related to permanent resident applications from January 1, 2000, to February 18, 2025. (3) all DNA results returned to the IRCC related to permanent resident applications from January 1, 2000, to February 18, 2025. (4) the total number of individuals granted permanent residency without providing DNA results despite DNA being requested from January 1, 2000, to February 18, 2025. (5) the total number of DNA test results that are held by the IRCC or by any other repository that collected DNA for immigration purposes from January 1, 2000, to February 18, 2025. Please provide copies of agreements between the IRCC and any databases that store DNA and/or sequence information on behalf of the IRCC. Please provide a list of national databanks that the IRCC has provided genetic information to, including the National DNA Data Bank of Canada and any related projects or pilot projects to whom IRCC provided genetic information. Please provide copies of agreements between the IRCC and organizations with whom the IRCC shares genetic information, as well as a list of the organizations to whom IRCC provided genetic information. Please provide copies of internal reports that were generated regarding all the aforementioned. Please provide copies of retention and disposal policies regarding the collection and uses of genetic information. Mark all Cabinet Confidences as non-relevant. | 81 pages | Source: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00032 - Canadian Human Rights Commission
item.description: Aggregate statistical information relating to complaints received by the Canadian Human Rights Commission in which federal departments or agencies are named as respondents. Specifically, I am requesting: 1. Complaint volumes by organization: the total number of complaints received, by fiscal year, for the past ten fiscal years (April 1, 2016, to February 10, 2026, inclusive), broken down by named federal department or agency respondent, including but not limited to the Canada Border Services Agency. 2. Segmentation by complainant type (where tracked) Where available, a breakdown of those complaints by complainant category, such as: current employees, former employees, members of the public, other government departments or agencies, and other external parties. 3. Complaint subject matter (where tracked) Any aggregate breakdown by allegation category (e.g., discrimination, harassment, disability, gender, race, reprisal, etc.). 4. Outcomes (aggregate only) Aggregate outcomes by department or agency and fiscal year (e.g., dismissed, mediated, settled, referred to Tribunal, ongoing). 5. Existing reporting products Any statistical summaries, dashboards, annual reports, or internal tracking documents that present or summarize these complaint volumes or trends by department or agency. This request is for aggregate and statistical information only. | 4 pages | Source: Canadian Human Rights Commission

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00031 - Canadian Human Rights Commission
item.description: Aggregate and statistical records relating to complaints filed by current or former Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) employees with the Canadian Human Rights Commission. Specifically: 1. The total number of complaints filed with the Canadian Human Rights Commission by current or former CBSA employees, by fiscal year, for the past ten (10) fiscal years to present (April 1st 2016 to February 10th, 2026). 2. Any available breakdown by complaint type or allegation category (e.g., discrimination, harassment, gender, disability, reprisal, etc.); 3. The status or outcome (if applicable) of those complaints where tracked (e.g., dismissed, settled, referred to Tribunal, ongoing); 4. Any internal reports, dashboards, summaries, briefing notes, or presentations prepared for management relating to the volume, trends, or risks associated with employee human rights complaints; 5. Any analyses, trend assessments, or risk evaluations regarding increases or patterns in CHRC complaints involving CBSA employees; 6. Any policies, procedures, or guidance documents addressing how CBSA tracks, manages, or responds to human rights complaints filed with the Canadian Human Rights Commission. This request is for aggregate and statistical information only. | 3 pages | Source: Canadian Human Rights Commission

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2025-00026 - Canadian Human Rights Commission
item.description:
This request seeks records pertaining to the CHRC’s policy, criteria, and operational procedures for the exercise of its discretionary power to reopen closed complaint files on its own initiative.
The request is framed by a recent and material example of this power’s application: the Commission’s decision to reopen a series of files concerning the Temporary Public Policy for out-of-status construction workers in the Greater Toronto Area. This action underscores the necessity of public scrutiny into the lawful and consistent application of this significant power.
To elucidate the institutional framework governing this discretionary power, we respectfully request the following categories of records: 1. Policy and Operational Framework Records: All records that define, establish, or discuss the criteria, process, and legal authority for the CHRC to reopen a closed complaint file suo motu (on its own initiative). This includes, but is not limited to: operational directives; procedural manuals; policy statements; briefing notes; memoranda; reports that analyze the scope, limits, or application of this power; training materials, presentations, or guidance documents provided to staff or commissioners; records that can be referenced as “transparency and accountability” with specific rationale for reopening files.
2. Records Specific to the Recent Exercise of Power: All records that discuss, analyze, or authorize the specific decision to reopen files related to the Temporary Public Policy for out-of-status construction workers in the GTA. This includes, but is not limited to: internal correspondence; emails; memoranda; briefing notes; records documenting the deliberation, rationale, and approval process for this decision, including records sent to or from related to temporary public policy for out-of-status construction workers in the Greater Toronto Area; records containing legal advice, risk analysis, or policy considerations pertaining to this specific action.
Timeframe: from November 14, 2020, to November 14, 2025. | 74 pages | Source: Canadian Human Rights Commission

item.title: [38956] 2026-04 - A-2021-00414 - Correctional Service of Canada
item.description: The designation of secure areas standing orders for the 43 correctional institutions for which the Correctional Service of Canada is responsible. | 214 pages | Source: Correctional Service of Canada
